hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: If This, Then That -- First Conditional
lesson.menu_desc: Ana plans a Friday rollout with a developer and does a quick risk analysis -- talking about cause and effect in testing with the first conditional (If the load is high, the app will slow down) -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 11 -- If This, Then That
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 11 -- If This, Then That

listenings.text: Welcome to risk-based testing. The idea is simple: before every rollout, you think in cause and effect. If a part of the system is important and fragile, you test it first. Ask yourself one question for each feature: if this breaks, what happens next? If the answer is we lose money, or the whole app goes down, that feature is high risk, so you test it hard. Watch the dependencies too. If one service depends on another, and the first one fails, the failure will cascade. And always think about load. If too many users arrive at the same time, a weak part becomes a bottleneck, and the response times climb. A good tester does not test everything equally. You follow the risk. If you find the dangerous conditions early, you will fix them before your users ever see them.

Hi team, a quick update on Friday's rollout before the stand-up. I finished the load test this morning, and I have good news and one condition. The good news: if the traffic stays under five thousand users, the new feature handles it easily. The condition: if we go over that threshold, the main service starts to time out. So here is the plan. We keep the fallback on. If the main service times out, the app will switch to the backup automatically, and the user will just see a small delay. But please, if the numbers climb above five thousand on Friday, ping me right away, because if we ignore it, the errors will cascade. If everything looks calm by noon, we will do the full rollout. Thanks, everyone.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana does a quick risk analysis before a rollout. First, she lists the main features and their dependencies. Then, for each one, she asks what happens if it fails. After that, she runs a load test to find the threshold. Next, she adds a fallback for the highest-risk part. Finally, she reports the conditions to the team before the rollout.
